sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=55, col=8).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Clicking (15, 48) had NO real gameplay effect - only HUD timer changed - Only clicking the white cross at (53, 10) caused a real gameplay change - I've tried: ACTION7 (x2), white cross, blue circle, gray dot, top magenta square, moved magenta square, gray 3x3 center - The white cross was the only interactive target - Need to find what else is interactive - maybe the bottom gray dots or the top charcoal bar
- Plan: Try clicking on the bottom-most gray dot at (55, 8) and (57, 6), and also try the top charcoal bar area.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking (55, 8) caused gameplay changes:
1. The magenta square moved from (52, 54, 9, 11) to (54, 56, 7, 9) - it moved down-left along the path
2. A new gray dot appeared at (53, 53, 10, 10) - this is where the magenta square was before
So clicking on a gray dot near the magenta square causes the magenta square to move to the next position along the path (towards the bottom-left). The old position becomes a gray dot again.
The path goes from the blue circle at top-right to the bottom-left corner. The magenta square is moving along this path towards the bottom-left.
Now I need to understand the goal. The path has gray dots at positions:
- (17, 46), (19, 44), (21, 42), (23, 40), (25, 38), (27, 36), (29, 34), (31, 32), (33, 30), (35, 28), (37, 26), (39, 24), (41, 22), (43, 20), (45, 18), (47, 16), (49, 14), (51, 12), (53, 10), (55, 8), (57, 6)
The magenta square is now at (54-56, 7-9). The next gray dot is at (57, 6). Maybe I need to keep clicking to move it all the way to the end of the path?
But wait, there's also the top magenta square at (4-6, 30-32). What's its role?
Let me think about the game mechanics:
1. Initial state: white cross at (52-54, 9-11), magenta square at (58-60, 3-5)
2. Clicking the white cross moved the magenta square there
3. Clicking the gray dot at (55, 8) moved the magenta square from (52-54, 9-11) to (54-56, 7-9)
So the mechanic seems to be: clicking on a gray dot near a magenta square moves that magenta square along the path. The direction seems to be towards the bottom-left (away from the blue circle).
But this doesn't make sense for a puzzle game - why would I move the magenta square away from the blue circle? Unless the goal is to move it all the way to the end of the path and then something happens.
Or maybe I need to click on gray dots in a specific order to move the magenta square along the path towards the blue circle? Let me try clicking on the gray dot at (51, 12) which is closer to the blue circle.
Wait, let me reconsider. The magenta square moved from (52-54, 9-11) to (54-56, 7-9). This is further along the path towards the bottom-left. But I clicked on (55, 8) which is also further along the path. So maybe clicking on a gray dot moves the nearest magenta square to that position?
Let me try clicking on (57, 6) to see if the magenta square moves there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- Clicking (55, 8) moved the magenta square from (52-54, 9-11) to (54-56, 7, 9)
- The old position became a gray dot at (53, 10)
- The magenta square moves along the gray dot path when clicking nearby gray dots
- The direction seems to be towards the bottom-left (away from blue circle)
- Need to understand if the goal is to move it to the end of the path
Plan: Try clicking on the next gray dot (57, 6) to continue moving the magenta square along the path.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the next gray dot at (57, 6) to see if the magenta square moves there
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 6}])
print(f"Click (57,6): board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

# Compare magenta squares
before_p = []
for n in before.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        before_p.append(r)

after_p = []
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        after_p.append(r)

print(f"Magenta before: {before_p}")
print(f"Magenta after: {after_p}")

# Check gray dots
before_g = []
for n in before.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        before_g.append(r)

after_g = []
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        after_g.append(r)

print(f"Gray dots before: {sorted(before_g)}")
print(f"Gray dots after: {sorted(after_g)}")

# Check if level completed
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
